summary: This is a brief follow-up post where Joel references his earlier Strategy Letter I, in which he used Wordsworth.com as an example of a company that failed despite being an early mover in online bookselling. Joel notes that someone from Wordsworth wrote back to respond to his characterization, and he links to their response. He also mentions a friend opening a small bookstore with an accompanying blog.
key_points: Early movers without lock-in and network effects can still lose to later entrants like Amazon
Wordsworth.com preceded Amazon by two years but failed to achieve widespread recognition
The post demonstrates Joel's practice of engaging with and linking to reader responses
money_quotes: If you're going into a market with no existing competition, lock-in, and network effects, you better use the Amazon model, or you're going the way of Wordsworth.com, which started two years before Amazon, and nobody's ever heard of them.
key_insight: Being first to market means nothing without a strategy for lock-in and network effects, as demonstrated by Wordsworth.com losing to the later-arriving Amazon.
